content: What does Paul exhort the Corinthian church about division?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 1:10: Be united, no divisions, of the same mind and the same judgment.
options: Avoid meeting one another
Be united in the same mind
Each group follow a different teacher
Form your own group

content: Who gives the growth in the work of the Lord?
explanation: 1 Cor 3:6-7: Paul planted, Apollos watered, but God gave the growth.
options: God
Peter
Paul
Apollos

content: What is the believer's body called?
explanation: 1 Cor 6:19: Your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit.
options: A temple of the Holy Spirit
An unclean thing
A tabernacle
Solomon's temple

content: What does God do when believers are tempted?
explanation: 1 Cor 10:13: He is faithful and will not let you be tempted beyond your ability, but provides a way of escape.
options: Abandons them
Punishes them immediately
Increases the temptation
Provides a way of escape

content: According to 1 Cor 13, which of the following is a quality of love?
explanation: 1 Cor 13:4-7 describes love.
options: Patient and kind
Arrogant
Envious
Boastful

content: What is the core of the gospel according to 1 Cor 15?
explanation: 1 Cor 15:3-4 presents the core of the gospel.
options: Building the temple
Fasting and prayer
Christ died, was buried, and rose on the third day
Keeping the Law

content: Why is the message of the cross seen as folly to those who are perishing?
explanation: 1 Cor 1:18: The cross is the power of God to those who are being saved.
options: They do not perceive God's power
Lack of evidence
Contrary to tradition
Lack of philosophy

content: By whom are spiritual things understood?
explanation: 1 Cor 2:14: The natural person does not accept the things of the Spirit; they must be understood through the Spirit.
options: Those who have the Holy Spirit
Intellectuals
The experienced
The morally upright

content: What lesson does "a little leaven leavens the whole lump" teach the church?
explanation: 1 Cor 5:6-8: Cleanse out the old leaven, that you may be a new lump.
options: Maintain outward form
Tolerate sin
Absolute freedom
Purity and discipline

content: How does Paul advise about marriage and singleness?
explanation: 1 Cor 7:7: Each has his own gift from God.
options: Each according to his own gift
Everyone should marry
Everyone should remain single
Based on financial circumstances

content: Regarding food offered to idols, what principle is set forth?
explanation: 1 Cor 8:1-13: Knowledge puffs up, but love builds up; do not become a stumbling block.
options: Love builds up; do not cause a stumbling block
Freedom is supreme
Just pray before eating
Knowledge is more important

content: Why does Paul become all things to all people?
explanation: 1 Cor 9:22-23: That by all means I might save some.
options: To receive a tithe
To save some
To gain honor
To receive praise

content: What is the purpose of the Lord's Supper according to 1 Cor 11?
explanation: 1 Cor 11:24-26: Do this in remembrance of me... proclaim the Lord's death until He comes.
options: To practice Judaism
To remember the Lord and proclaim His death
To share food
Social bonding

content: Who distributes spiritual gifts to each person?
explanation: 1 Cor 12:11: One and the same Spirit works all things, distributing to each as He wills.
options: The apostles
The pastors
The Holy Spirit, as He wills
The congregation

content: What is the principle for governing corporate worship?
explanation: 1 Cor 14:26-33: God is not a God of confusion, but of peace.
options: No need for interpretation
The more tongues, the better
All things should be done for edification, in order
Whoever wants to speak speaks

content: What does the running-the-race image teach the disciple?
explanation: 1 Cor 9:24-27: I discipline my body and keep it under control.
options: Boast about the prize
Must defeat others
Self-control and discipline to win an imperishable crown
Need physical strength

content: Why is love greater than faith and hope?
explanation: 1 Cor 13:13: Now faith, hope, and love abide; but the greatest of these is love.
options: Because it is easy to practice
Because it is praised more
Because love endures forever
Because it is less difficult

content: What are the consequences if Christ has not been raised?
explanation: 1 Cor 15:14-19: Your faith is futile, you are still in your sins.
options: The Law is futile
Prayer is futile
Missions is futile
Faith is futile and we are still in our sins

content: What is the central concluding exhortation of 1 Corinthians?
explanation: 1 Cor 16:13-14: Be watchful, stand firm in the faith, be strong, and let all you do be done in love.
options: Avoid persecution
Seek the greater gifts
Meet together regularly
Stand firm, be strong; do all in love

content: In 1 Corinthians 13:4, "Love is patient." What does the Greek word for "patient" mean?
explanation: Makrothumeo (μακροθυμέω) means "long-suffering," not merely enduring but patient over time.
options: Hupomeno - To endure
Makrothumeo - Long-suffering
Anechomai - To bear with
Kartereo - To persevere

content: In 1 Corinthians 15:55, "O death, where is your victory?" What does the Greek word for "victory" mean?
explanation: Nikos (νῖκος) means "victory" — death has been defeated by Jesus's resurrection.
options: Trophe - Food
Nikos - Victory
Broma - Food
Stinger - Sting

content: According to 1 Corinthians 13, what does love begin with?
explanation: 1 Cor 13:4 - Love is patient, love is kind. Patience and kindness are the foundation of true love.
options: Giving
Forgiveness
Sacrifice
Patience and kindness

content: Among faith, hope, and love that abide, which is the greatest?
explanation: 1 Cor 13:13 - Love is greatest because it is the very nature of God (1 John 4:8).
options: Faith
Love
Hope
All three are equal

content: According to 1 Cor 15:3-4, the gospel can be summarized in how many essentials?
explanation: 1 Cor 15:3-4 - The gospel: Christ died for our sins (1), was buried (2), and rose on the third day (3) according to the Scriptures.
options: 7 sacraments
5 binding rules
3 truths: Christ died for sins, was buried, and rose on the third day
1 thing: believe in God

content: The message of the cross is folly to those perishing, but to us it is what?
explanation: 1 Cor 1:18 - The cross is folly to the world but the power of salvation to those who believe.
options: Tradition
Mystery
The power of God
Knowledge

content: 1 Cor 2:9: "What no eye has seen, nor ear heard, nor the heart of man imagined" — refers to what?
explanation: What God has prepared surpasses human imagination.
options: Miracles
The gospel
What God has prepared for those who love Him
Heaven

content: 1 Cor 3:16: What are our bodies?
explanation: The body is the temple where the Holy Spirit dwells. We must keep it holy.
options: An offering
An instrument of God
A temple of the Holy Spirit
A jar of clay

content: What does Paul compare the believer's life to?
explanation: 1 Cor 9:24 - The life of faith is like a race: it requires discipline and effort to reach the goal.
options: A race; run to obtain the prize
Trade
Farming
Battle

content: What does 1 Cor 10:13 promise about temptation?
explanation: God is faithful: He will not let you be tempted beyond your ability and always provides a way of escape.
options: Temptation will end
God will not let us be tempted beyond our ability and always provides a way of escape
There will be no temptation
We must endure on our own

content: What does Paul compare the church to?
explanation: 1 Cor 12:12-14 - Each has different gifts but all are essential, like members of one body.
options: A building
A body with many members, each member needed
A vineyard
An army

content: If one has gifts but lacks love, what does Paul say he is like?
explanation: 1 Cor 13:1 - All gifts are worthless without love.
options: A strong person
A scholar
A noisy gong or a clanging cymbal
An angel

content: 1 Cor 15:58 concludes: Be steadfast, abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that your labor in the Lord is not ___
explanation: Every labor done for the Lord has eternal value and is never wasted.
options: In vain
Late
Forgotten
Lost

content: 1 Cor 10:31: Whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all for the ___ of God
explanation: Every act, however small, can be an act that glorifies God.
options: Profit
Reputation
Joy
Glory

content: 1 Cor 16:13-14 calls believers to do everything in what?
explanation: Be watchful, stand firm, be courageous, be strong — but do everything in love.
options: Hope
Fear
Love
Righteousness

content: According to 1 Corinthians 1:1-2, Paul writes this letter to whom?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 1:2 — 'To the church of God that is in Corinth, to those sanctified in Christ Jesus, called to be saints.' Paul wrote from Ephesus around AD 55 during his third missionary journey.
options: To the church in Rome (per the textual narrative)
To the believers in Ephesus
To the Church of God in Corinth — those sanctified in Christ Jesus
To the church in Philippi

content: According to 1 Corinthians 1:30, what has God made Christ Jesus to be for us?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 1:30 — 'And because of him you are in Christ Jesus, who became to us wisdom from God, righteousness and sanctification and redemption.' A condensed Christological verse — Christ is EVERYTHING the believer needs.
options: Merely a moral example
Merely a teacher of the law
Merely a giver of material blessing
Wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, and redemption

content: According to 1 Corinthians 11:1, Paul urges believers to imitate whom?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 11:1 — 'Be imitators of me, as I am of Christ.' Paul is not self-promoting — he is an example only as and when he reflects Christ. This is the discipleship principle: Christian modeling flows from following Christ.
options: Imitate the other apostles
Imitate Paul as he imitates Christ
Imitate the Old Testament prophets
Imitate angels
tags: Imitate Paul as he imitates Christ

content: According to 1 Corinthians 13:11, when Paul was a child, what did he do?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 13:11 — 'When I was a child, I spoke like a child, I thought like a child, I reasoned like a child. When I became a man, I gave up childish ways.' Illustration: present gifts (tongues, knowledge) will cease when 'the perfect' (Christ's return) comes — but love remains.
options: Spoke like a child, thought like a child, reasoned like a child
Had full understanding from the start
Lacked the capacity for emotion
Had the wisdom of a grown man

content: According to 1 Corinthians 13:12, how do we see now?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 13:12 — 'For now we see in a mirror dimly, but then face to face. Now I know in part; then I shall know fully, even as I have been fully known.' Ancient mirrors were polished metal — giving a dim reflection. When Christ returns, our knowing will be perfect.
options: Face to face clearly
Not at all (per the textual narrative)
Dimly, as in a mirror
As clearly as in broad daylight

content: According to 1 Corinthians 14:1, what is the top priority, and which gift should believers desire?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:1 — 'Pursue love, and earnestly desire the spiritual gifts, especially that you may prophesy.' Love (ch 13) is 'the more excellent way' (12:31) — the foundation for all gifts. Prophecy is preferred over tongues because it builds up the church (14:3-4).
options: Pursue knowledge; desire tongues
Pursue love; earnestly desire spiritual gifts, especially prophecy
Pursue wealth; desire healing
Pursue peace; desire miracles

content: According to 1 Corinthians 15:20, Christ has been raised from the dead and become what?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:20 — 'But in fact Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep.' 'Firstfruits' (aparche) — an OT metaphor (Lev 23:10) foreshadowing the full harvest. Christ rose first → guaranteeing believers' resurrection.
options: An angel (according to the text's framing)
A judge (per the textual narrative)
The firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep
A prophet (as described in the passage)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 15:57, why do believers thank God?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:57 — 'But thanks be to God, who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ.' The climax of the resurrection argument — death swallowed up in victory (15:54). This grounds the next exhortation: 'be steadfast, immovable' (15:58).
options: For granting wealth
For granting health
For granting wisdom
For giving us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ

content: According to 1 Corinthians 1:11-13, who told Paul about the divisions in Corinth, and what were the 4 factions called?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 1:11-12 — 'It has been reported to me by Chloe's people that there is quarreling among you. What I mean is that each one of you says, I follow Paul, or I follow Apollos, or I follow Cephas, or I follow Christ.' Paul exposes personality cults. Verse 13 asks pointedly: 'Is Christ divided?'
options: Stephanas — Paul / Apollos / Timothy / Christ
Chloe's people — Paul / Apollos / Cephas (Peter) / Christ
Phoebe — Paul / Titus / Cephas / Barnabas
Aquila — Peter / James / Apollos / Paul

content: According to 1 Corinthians 1:23, Paul preaches Christ crucified — what is this to Jews and Greeks?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 1:23 — 'But we preach Christ crucified, a stumbling block to Jews and folly to Gentiles.' Jews expected a conquering Messiah, not a crucified one; Greeks revered philosophy and saw a crucified Christ as foolish. But to those called, He is 'the power of God and the wisdom of God' (1:24).
options: Wisdom to both (per the textual narrative)
Salvation to both (as described in the passage)
A stumbling block to Jews and folly to Greeks
Victory to both (according to the text's framing)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 1:26-29, whom does God choose, and to what end?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 1:27-29 — 'God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise... so that no human being might boast in the presence of God.' The Corinthian church had few wise/powerful/noble. Theological purpose: glory belongs to God alone, not to people.
options: The foolish, weak, and lowly — so that no one may boast before him
The wise and powerful of this world
The rich and educated (according to the text's framing)
Those of noble birth (per the textual narrative)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 2:1-5, Paul came to Corinth WITHOUT what, so their faith would rest on what?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 2:4-5 — 'My speech and my message were not in plausible words of wisdom, but in demonstration of the Spirit and of power, so that your faith might not rest in the wisdom of men but in the power of God.' Paul resolved to 'know nothing among you except Jesus Christ and him crucified' (2:2).
options: Without money — faith rests on possessions
Without political power — faith rests on organization
Without signs and wonders — faith rests on emotion
Without lofty speech or human wisdom — faith rests on God's power

content: According to 1 Corinthians 2:8, if the rulers of this age had known God's hidden wisdom, what would they NOT have done?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 2:8 — 'None of the rulers of this age understood this, for if they had, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory.' 'The Lord of glory' — high Christology: Christ is God's glory (cf. Jas 2:1). God's wisdom was hidden from the world — revealed only by the Spirit (2:10).
options: Would not believe (according to the text's framing)
Would not teach (per the textual narrative)
Would NOT have crucified the Lord of glory
Would not have prospered

content: According to 1 Corinthians 2:16, what does Paul affirm about believers (concluding from Isa 40:13)?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 2:16 — 'For who has understood the mind of the Lord so as to instruct him? But we have the mind of Christ.' Quoting Isaiah 40:13 (LXX). The Holy Spirit, who searches the depths of God (2:10), dwells in believers — so we have access to Christ's mind.
options: We have the wisdom of the world
We have the mind of Christ
We have the strength of angels
We have the authority of kings

content: According to 1 Corinthians 3:1-4, why can Paul not address the Corinthians as spiritual people?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 3:1-3 — 'I could not address you as spiritual people, but as people of the flesh, as infants in Christ. I fed you with milk, not solid food, for you were not ready... when one says, I follow Paul, and another, I follow Apollos — are you not being merely human?' Division is the proof they are still fleshly.
options: Because they are still infants of the flesh — only milk, not solid food
Because they are not yet saved
Because they are pagans (per the textual narrative)
Because they have no gifts (according to the text's framing)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 3:10-15, by what will each person's work be tested, and what 6 materials are listed?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 3:12-13 — 'If anyone builds on the foundation with gold, silver, precious stones, wood, hay, straw — each one's work will become manifest; the Day will disclose it, because it will be revealed by fire.' 3 fire-proof materials (gold/silver/stones), 3 combustibles (wood/hay/straw). The saved but burned-up worker is 'saved as through fire' (3:15) — keeps salvation but loses reward.
options: By water; wood, stone, clay, wheat, barley, oil
By wind; cloud, dust, dew, rain, snow, ice
By time; gold, silver, copper, iron, lead, zinc
By fire; gold, silver, precious stones — wood, hay, straw

content: According to 1 Corinthians 4:1-2, how should Paul and the apostles be regarded, and what virtue is required of them?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 4:1-2 — 'This is how one should regard us, as servants of Christ and stewards of the mysteries of God. Moreover, it is required of stewards that they be found faithful.' A steward (oikonomos) does not own — only manages on the master's behalf. Apostles do not own the Gospel — only transmit it faithfully.
options: As masters — must be wise (per the textual narrative)
As servants of Christ and stewards of God's mysteries — must be found faithful
As patrons — must be generous (according to the text's framing)
As lawgivers — must be strict (as described in the passage)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 5:1-5, what does Paul tell the Corinthian church to do with the man living with his stepmother?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 5:5 — 'You are to deliver this man to Satan for the destruction of the flesh, so that his spirit may be saved in the day of the Lord.' Discipline's purpose: NOT vengeance — soul rescue. The church was arrogant (5:2) instead of mourning — Paul's rebuke.
options: Ignore, forgive, leave it to God (as described in the passage)
Take it to Roman court (according to the text's framing)
Remove from the community — hand over to Satan, that the flesh be destroyed, that the spirit be saved on the Lord's day
Beat him then restore (per the textual narrative)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 5:9-13, Paul does NOT mean avoiding outsiders — whom is he saying not to associate with?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 5:11-12 — 'I am writing to you not to associate with anyone who bears the name of brother if he is guilty of sexual immorality or greed, or is an idolater, reviler, drunkard, or swindler... is it not those inside the church whom you are to judge?' Paul distinguishes: believers must LIVE IN the unbelieving world but NOT tolerate sin INSIDE the church.
options: Don't associate with the poor (as described in the passage)
Don't associate with pagans (according to the text's framing)
Don't associate with Jews (per the textual narrative)
Don't associate with anyone called a brother who is sexually immoral, greedy, an idolater, a reviler, a drunkard, or a swindler

content: According to 1 Corinthians 6:1-8, what does Paul rebuke the Corinthians for, and what is his main reason?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 6:1-3 — 'When one of you has a grievance against another, does he dare go to law before the unrighteous instead of the saints? Do you not know that the saints will judge the world? Do you not know that we are to judge angels?' Better to 'be wronged' (6:7) than to disgrace Christ's name before pagan courts.
options: Lawsuits against each other before unbelieving courts — why not have saints judge? saints will judge angels
Not participating in Roman politics (as described in the passage)
Not honoring secular courts (according to the text's framing)
Tax evasion (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 6:9-11, after listing those who will NOT inherit God's kingdom, what does Paul affirm to the believers?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 6:11 — 'And such were some of you. But you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God.' Three past tenses — apologomai (washed), hagiazō (sanctified), dikaioō (justified). Gospel transformation: 'such WERE some of you.'
options: None of you ever sinned (according to the text's framing)
You must keep OT law (per the textual narrative)
You were washed, sanctified, justified in Jesus Christ's name and by God's Spirit
You must do good works to be saved

content: According to 1 Corinthians 6:12, what principle does Paul state about freedom in Christ?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 6:12 — 'All things are lawful for me, but not all things are helpful. All things are lawful for me, but I will not be dominated by anything.' Possibly a Corinthian slogan — Paul does not deny freedom but limits it: HELPFULNESS and NON-ENSLAVEMENT. Key verse for Christian freedom theology.
options: All things are forbidden (according to the text's framing)
All things are lawful — but not all things are helpful, and I will not be enslaved by anything
Absolute freedom without limits (as described in the passage)
Must keep Mosaic law (per the textual narrative)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 7:10-11, what is the command from the Lord for married believers?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 7:10-11 — 'To the married I give this charge (not I, but the Lord): the wife should not separate from her husband (but if she does, she should remain unmarried or else be reconciled to her husband), and the husband should not divorce his wife.' Paul cites Jesus' own words (Mt 19:6 — 'what God has joined together, let not man separate').
options: The wife must not separate from her husband — if she does, remain unmarried or be reconciled; husband must not divorce his wife
Each free to divorce at will (per the textual narrative)
Wife must absolutely submit even to abuse (as described in the passage)
Husband may have multiple wives (according to the text's framing)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 7:29-31, what does Paul argue about the 'shortness of time' and earthly relationships?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 7:29-31 — 'This is what I mean, brothers: the appointed time has grown very short. From now on, let those who have wives live as though they had none, and those who mourn as though they were not mourning... For the present form of this world is passing away.' Not abandoning duty, but holding eternal focus — all things are temporary in light of Christ's return.
options: Avoid the world entirely (as described in the passage) (as the text indicates)
Become celibate monks (according to the text's framing) (per the original content)
Sell all possessions (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)
Those with wives as not having; mourners as not mourning; rejoicers as not rejoicing; buyers as not possessing — for this world's form is passing away

content: According to 1 Corinthians 9:1-12, what right does Paul affirm he HAS as an apostle — but what did he NOT do?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 9:11-12 — 'If we have sown spiritual things among you, is it too much if we reap material things from you?... Nevertheless, we have not made use of this right, but we endure anything rather than put an obstacle in the way of the gospel of Christ.' Paul forfeits a legitimate right for the Gospel's advance.
options: Right to steal — but didn't (according to the text's framing)
Right to eat, take a believing wife, receive material support from churches — but did NOT use that right so the Gospel would not be hindered
Right to rule — but didn't take power (as described in the passage)
Right to heal — but didn't (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 10:1-4, what did all the Israelite fathers in the wilderness experience, and who was the 'spiritual rock'?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 10:2-4 — 'All were baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea, and all ate the same spiritual food, and all drank the same spiritual drink. For they drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and the Rock was Christ.' A stunning pre-incarnate Christ — Christ was already present and provider for His people in the wilderness.
options: All escaped Egypt — the rock is Moses (according to the text's framing)
All ate manna — the rock is an angel (per the textual narrative)
All baptized into Moses (through cloud and sea), all ate the same spiritual food and drank the same spiritual drink — the rock that followed them was Christ
All saw Sinai — the rock is the nation (as described in the passage)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 10:14-22, why CAN'T believers share both the Lord's table and the table of demons?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 10:21 — 'You cannot drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of demons. You cannot partake of the table of the Lord and the table of demons.' Idol-feast participation — even by an unbeliever in idols — still creates 'koinōnia' (fellowship) with demons. The Lord's Supper creates real communion with Christ's body and blood (10:16) — incompatible with demonic communion.
options: Because drinking the Lord's cup and the cup of demons is incompatible communion — you cannot serve both the Lord and demons
Because the law forbids (according to the text's framing)
Because it costs money (per the textual narrative)
Because the institution doesn't allow it (as described in the passage)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 11:27-32, what is the consequence of unworthy participation in the Lord's Supper, and what should believers do first?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 11:28-30 — 'Let a person examine himself, then, and so eat of the bread and drink of the cup. For anyone who eats and drinks without discerning the body eats and drinks judgment on himself. That is why many of you are weak and ill, and some have died.' 'Sleep' = died. The Corinthian church had treated the Supper carelessly, eating drunkenly (11:21).
options: Loss of salvation — must confess to a priest
Forgotten by God — must offer sacrifice
No consequence — no examination needed
Guilty of the Lord's body and blood; many are weak, sick, and dying; must examine oneself first

content: According to 1 Corinthians 12:1-3, what is a clear sign that a person is NOT speaking by the Holy Spirit, and what confession comes ONLY by the Spirit?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:3 — 'Therefore I want you to understand that no one speaking in the Spirit of God ever says, Jesus is accursed! and no one can say, Jesus is Lord, except in the Holy Spirit.' This is the SIMPLEST spiritual test: attitude toward Christ. 'Jesus is Lord' (Kyrios Iesous) is the core Christian confession.
options: Not speaking tongues — only Spirit gives tongues
No gifts — only Spirit gives gifts (per the textual narrative)
Saying 'Anathema (cursed) Jesus' is NOT from the Spirit; confessing 'Jesus is Lord' comes ONLY from the Holy Spirit
Not crying — only Spirit causes tears (according to the text's framing)
tags: Jesus is Lord — by Spirit only

content: According to 1 Corinthians 12:13, how have believers from all backgrounds been united?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:13 — 'For in one Spirit we were all baptized into one body — Jews or Greeks, slaves or free — and all were made to drink of one Spirit.' 'Baptism in the Spirit' = the Lord Jesus immersing the believer into the Spirit's life at conversion. The Church = One Body transcending every racial/class barrier.
options: By learning a common doctrine (as described in the passage)
By ONE Spirit we were all baptized into ONE Body — Jew or Greek, slave or free — and all made to drink of ONE Spirit
By common nationality (per the textual narrative)
By common social class (according to the text's framing)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 14:1-5, why does Paul prefer prophecy over tongues in the church?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:4-5 — 'The one who speaks in a tongue builds up himself, but the one who prophesies builds up the church. Now I want you all to speak in tongues, but even more to prophesy... the one who prophesies is greater than the one who speaks in tongues, unless someone interprets, so that the church may be built up.' Focus: BENEFIT to the Body — not private experience.
options: Tongues are not real (as described in the passage)
Prophecy is easier (according to the text's framing)
The prophet builds (oikodomeō) the CHURCH; the tongue-speaker builds himself only — unless interpreted
Tongues are noisy (per the textual narrative)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 15:5-8, after the resurrection, to whom did Christ appear in the order Paul lists?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:5-8 — 'He appeared to Cephas, then to the twelve. Then he appeared to more than five hundred brothers at one time... Then he appeared to James, then to all the apostles. Last of all, as to one untimely born, he appeared also to me.' This is the EARLIEST resurrection creed in the NT (~5 years post-Resurrection) — many eyewitnesses STILL LIVING when Paul wrote (~AD 55).
options: Only to the 12 (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)
Only to Mary Magdalene (according to the text's framing)
Only to Peter then the 12 — that's all (as described in the passage)
To Cephas (Peter), the 12, more than 500 brothers at once, James, the apostles, last to Paul as one untimely born

content: According to 1 Corinthians 1:30 in depth — what are the four specific functions of Christ listed (in ESV/RVV11 order)?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 1:30 — 'God made Christ Jesus our wisdom and our righteousness and sanctification and redemption.' 4 ordered functions: SOPHIA (wisdom = umbrella) → DIKAIOSYNĒ (justification = past) → HAGIASMOS (sanctification = present) → APOLYTRŌSIS (redemption = future glorification). Christ is the 'A to Z' of salvation.
options: Love, faith, hope, joy (per the textual narrative)
Wisdom from God, righteousness, sanctification, redemption
Peace, joy, faith, charity (as described in the passage)
Life, light, truth, way (according to the text's framing)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 2:14, how does the 'natural' (psychikos) person respond to spiritual things, and why?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 2:14 — 'The natural person does not accept the things of the Spirit of God, for they are folly to him, and he is not able to understand them because they are spiritually discerned.' Psychikos = 'soulish-natural' (lacking the indwelling Spirit). Spiritual understanding requires the Spirit — not natural ability.
options: Fully understands — but doesn't obey (according to the text's framing)
Partly understands — and tries to obey (as described in the passage)
Does NOT accept the things of the Spirit — sees them as folly; CANNOT understand because they are spiritually discerned
Believes naturally — without Spirit (per the textual narrative)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 3:11, what foundation does Paul declare NO ONE can lay other than?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 3:11 — 'For no one can lay a foundation other than that which is laid, which is Jesus Christ.' Different from Mt 16:18 ('Peter is the rock') — Paul clarifies: in church building, the FOUNDATION is Christ alone. Paul calls himself a 'wise master builder' laying foundation (3:10), but Christ IS the foundation.
options: No one can lay any foundation other than the one already laid — Jesus Christ
Paul is the foundation (per the textual narrative)
Apollos is the foundation (according to the text's framing)
Peter alone is the foundation (as described in the passage)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 3:16-17, who is the 'temple of God' (in chapter 3's context) — and how does this differ from 6:19?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 3:16-17 — 'Do you not know that you are God's temple and that God's Spirit dwells in you? If anyone destroys God's temple, God will destroy him.' 'You' = plural (THE CHURCH), and the context is division destroying the church = destroying God's temple. But 6:19 'your body' (singular distributive) = individual believer, in sexual immorality context.
options: Individual believer — same as 6:19 (as described in the passage) (as the text indicates)
Temple in Jerusalem — unrelated (according to the text's framing) (per the original content)
Only the apostles — special (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)
The CHURCH (community) is God's temple (3:16-17, division context); 6:19 says the INDIVIDUAL'S body is the temple of the Holy Spirit (sexual immorality context)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 4:7, what three rhetorical questions does Paul use to dismantle Corinthian pride?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 4:7 — 'For who sees anything different in you? What do you have that you did not receive? If then you received it, why do you boast as if you did not receive it?' Three questions shatter Corinthian pride — EVERYTHING they have (gifts, wisdom, position) IS A GIFT. Boasting denies the gift.
options: Who are you? Where from? Going where? (per the textual narrative)
Who makes you different? What do you have that you did not receive? If you received it, why boast as if not received?
What do you believe? What do you do? Where do you go? (as described in the passage)
Who called you? Sent you? Provides for you? (according to the text's framing)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 7:29 specifically, how does Paul say 'from now on' those with wives should live, based on what reason?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 7:29 — 'This is what I mean, brothers: the appointed time has grown very short. From now on, let those who have wives live as though they had none.' 'Synestalmenos' = compressed (perfect passive). NOT abandoning wife — but holding eternal focus over earthly relations. This is the 'hōs mē' (as if not) theology — living with eternal priority.
options: Live AS THOUGH they had NO wives — because 'time is short' (ho kairos synestalmenos estin)
Must abandon wife — need celibacy (according to the text's framing)
Take many wives — for descendants (as described in the passage)
Live normally — no change (per the textual narrative)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 10:11, why are the wilderness events of Israel recorded?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 10:11 — 'Now these things happened to them as an example, but they were written down for our instruction, on whom the end of the ages has come.' 'Typikōs' = type/example; 'telē tōn aiōnōn' = end of the ages. NT believers live in the last days — OT events function as WARNINGS for us.
options: To preserve culture (as described in the passage) (per the original content)
Only for Jews (according to the text's framing) (as the text indicates)
As examples (typikōs) for us — written for our instruction, on whom 'the end of the ages' has come (telē tōn aiōnōn)
As mythology (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 10:16-17, with what is the Lord's Supper described as koinōnia (fellowship), and why does 'one bread' prove 'one body'?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 10:16-17 — 'The cup of blessing that we bless, is it not a participation in the blood of Christ? The bread that we break, is it not a participation in the body of Christ? Because there is one bread, we who are many are one body, for we all partake of the one bread.' The Supper creates REAL communion with Christ — not mere symbol. 'One bread' (artos) = the singular source forming the body.
options: Fellowship with humanity — bread is just bread (according to the text's framing) (per the original content)
Fellowship with ancestors — no spiritual meaning (as described in the passage) (as the text indicates)
Fellowship with Paul — because he teaches (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence) (cross-referenced with related passages)
The cup of blessing = communion with Christ's blood; the broken bread = communion with Christ's body; because there is ONE BREAD, we who are many are ONE BODY — partaking of the same ONE BREAD

content: According to 1 Corinthians 11:30 (in the context of 11:27-32), what are the three consequences Paul cites for unworthy participation in the Lord's Supper?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 11:30 — 'That is why many of you are weak and ill, and some have died.' 3 levels: ASTHENĒS (weak) → ARRŌSTOS (sick) → KOIMĀSTHAI (asleep = dead). 'Sleep' is the NT euphemism for believers' death (cf. 1 Th 4:13). The Supper is NOT trifling — God disciplines through illness and death.
options: Loss of grace — banishment — isolation
Changed faith — changed religion — changed church
Many weak, many sick, not a few asleep (dead)
Lost job — lost money — lost friends

content: According to 1 Corinthians 12:8-10, what are the nine specific spiritual gifts listed (in order)?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:8-10 — '(1) Word of wisdom, (2) word of knowledge, (3) faith, (4) gifts of healing, (5) working of miracles, (6) prophecy, (7) distinguishing of spirits, (8) various kinds of tongues, (9) interpretation of tongues.' 9 gifts in 3 groups: revelation (1-2), power (3-5), speech (6-9). All from ONE Holy Spirit (12:11) — distributed as he wills.
options: Word of wisdom, word of knowledge, faith, gifts of healing, working of miracles, prophecy, distinguishing spirits, kinds of tongues, interpretation of tongues
Love, faith, hope, grace, peace, patience, joy, gentleness, self-control (per the textual narrative)
Apostle, prophet, evangelist, pastor, teacher, healer, helper, administrator, speaker
Wisdom, understanding, counsel, might, fear of the Lord, reverence, obedience, righteousness, kindness

content: According to 1 Corinthians 13:13 in depth — why is 'love' called the 'greatest' among the three abiding things (faith, hope, love)?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 13:13 — 'So now faith, hope, and love abide, these three; but the greatest of these is love.' Faith ends at meeting its object (cf. Heb 11:1); hope ends at receiving (Rom 8:24); but love is the very NATURE of God — never ending (13:8 'love never ends').
options: Because love is easiest to practice (as described in the passage) (as the text indicates)
Because faith and hope have ceased (according to the text's framing)
Because Paul favors love (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)
Because at Christ's return, faith becomes sight and hope becomes possession — but love CONTINUES forever (since God IS love, 1 Jn 4:8)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 14:22, the tongues and prophecy are signs for whom?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:22 — 'Thus tongues are a sign not for believers but for unbelievers, while prophecy is a sign not for unbelievers but for believers.' Quoting Isa 28:11-12 (14:21) — God speaks via 'foreign tongues' to unfaithful people as a JUDGMENT. Tongues function as judgment; prophecy as edification.
options: Tongues for believers; prophecy for unbelievers (in the story's sequence)
Tongues are a sign NOT for believers but for UNBELIEVERS; prophecy is for BELIEVERS, not unbelievers
Both for believers (per the textual narrative) (as described in the passage)
Both for unbelievers (according to the text's framing)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 15:24-26, who is the last enemy (eschatos echthros) to be destroyed, and when does Christ deliver the kingdom to the Father?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:24-26 — 'Then comes the end, when he delivers the kingdom to God the Father after destroying every rule and every authority and power. For he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet. The last enemy to be destroyed is death.' Mt 22:44/Ps 110:1 — enemies under feet; death is the LAST enemy — destroyed at the resurrection of all believers.
options: Satan — when Christ wins (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)
Sin — when believers are sanctified (as described in the passage)
Death — when all enemies are placed under Christ's feet, and then Christ delivers the kingdom to the Father
Darkness — when dawn breaks (according to the text's framing)

content: According to 1 Corinthians 15:45, how does Paul contrast 'first Adam' with 'last Adam' (eschatos Adam)?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:45 — 'The first man Adam became a living being; the last Adam became a life-giving spirit.' Quoting Gen 2:7. First Adam only RECEIVED life; Christ the second Adam GIVES life. Contrast: natural (psychikon) vs spiritual (pneumatikon) — the risen Christ is the pattern for the believer's resurrection body (15:49).
options: Both are ordinary humans (according to the text's framing) (as the text indicates)
First Adam is greater than the last (as described in the passage)
Last Adam is David (per the textual narrative) (in the story's sequence)
First Adam became 'a living soul' (psychēn zōsan); last Adam (Christ) became 'a life-giving spirit' (pneuma zōopoioun)
